steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage ends with "This suggests that ______" — it needs a logical inference from the evidence shown. The key evidence: Darwin's fall 1838 notebook labels the struggle-for-survival idea as "inference from Malthus," citing an 1798 economics essay, then his October 1838 notebook uses strikingly similar language ("grand crush of population"). The inference must explain what that sequence suggests.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'some entries date from 1838, others as late as 1858': states a date-range fact but draws no inference from the Malthus connection. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Darwin realized Malthus derived his ideas from animal behavior': reverses the direction of influence — Malthus influenced Darwin, not the other way around. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Darwin determined an economic postulate might apply to biology': directly matches the passage's evidence — Darwin borrowed an economic concept (Malthus on population outpacing production) and applied it to biological systems. ✓
  • Choice D — 'economists had previously articulated natural selection in the same terms': the passage shows Darwin adopted a Malthusian premise, not that economists had already stated natural selection. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
